title: Incidence and prevalence of extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis
subtitle: The estimated number of people living with [extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis](#dod:drug-resistant-tuberculosis) (prevalence) versus new cases each year (incidence).
caption:
Incidence and prevalence of extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis, World

The estimated number of people living with extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis (prevalence) versus new cases each year (incidence).

This line chart tracks the global burden of extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis from 1990 through 2023, measuring the estimated number of people living with the condition (prevalence) against the number of new cases diagnosed each year (incidence). The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 35,000 cases, while the horizontal axis spans the thirty-three-year period. Two distinct trend lines visualize the data: a blue line representing prevalence and an orange line representing incidence. Both metrics begin near zero in 1990 and follow a similar upward trajectory for the first decade. By 1995, both lines cross the 2,500 mark, with incidence rising slightly faster initially. The rate of increase accelerates significantly between 1995 and 2010. During this period, the prevalence line climbs steeply, reaching approximately 27,500 by 2010, while the incidence line rises to just over 20,000 in the same year. After 2010, the slope of both lines flattens, indicating a slower rate of growth compared to the previous decade. The prevalence line continues to rise steadily, reaching roughly 33,000 by 2023. The incidence line shows a more gradual increase in the final years, ending near 24,000. The data includes tuberculosis cases in HIV-negative people, with cases among people with HIV classified under HIV/AIDS. Throughout the entire timeline, the blue prevalence line remains above the orange incidence line, and the vertical distance between the two lines widens over time, showing that the total number of people living with the disease consistently exceeds the number of new annual cases.
